sound_type: sighing collective wind-sound -- aggregate sound of thousands of wings turning in unison during murmuration over tidal flats. NOT vocal. Collective phenomenon. E=3
ecosystem: Tidal flats, mudflats, coastal estuaries. Most abundant wintering shorebird on Pacific Coast. Massive winter flocks on Puget Sound.
season: Winter resident (October-April). Murmurations during winter tidal cycles.

coordinates.description: Dunlin flocks on Padilla Bay, Skagit Bay, and other Puget Sound tidal flats during winter. Massive murmurations.

taxonomy_note.description: SECOND SCOLOPACIDAE entry. Dunlin flock murmuration. Collective wing-sound rather than individual call. Thousands of small shorebirds wheeling as one. Predator-defense behavior. Tidal flat habitat.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Calidris from Greek 'kalidris' (gray-speckled sandpiper). alpina from Latin 'alpinus' (alpine) -- referencing breeding habitat. Dunlin from 'dunling' (little dun-colored bird).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Calidris -- sandpipers. ~24 species. Small to medium shorebirds. The largest sandpiper genus.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Second Scolopacidae. Murmuration as sound source. Collective rather than individual.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Collective wing-sound: broadband whoosh, no clear fundamental. Individual flight call: 2000-4000 Hz (thin, nasal).
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Collective: broadband. Individual kreee call: ~3000 Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Not applicable -- collective sound is broadband whoosh.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Murmuration: 1-15+ minutes continuous. Individual call: 0.3-0.5 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: Collective: continuous rushing/sighing wind-sound. Individual: thin nasal 'kreee' or 'treeee'.
song_metrics.note_types: Collective wing-sound (primary -- flock), kreee flight call (individual -- vocal), flock chatter (aggregate individual calls).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Collective: continuous, rising and falling with flock movements. No phrase structure -- ambient and atmospheric.
song_metrics.timbre: Collective: rushing, sighing, atmospheric. Like wind or breath. Individual: thin, nasal, reedy, slightly buzzy.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate, atmospheric, proximity-dependent. Gentle collective sigh.
song_metrics.tempo: Collective: continuous. Wing-beat rate ~8-10 Hz per individual. Aggregate creates continuous wash.
song_metrics.context: Predator response, tidal displacement, winter flock behavior. Murmuration over tidal flats.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Collective sound determined by flock dynamics rather than vocal learning.
song_metrics.key_property: COLLECTIVE WING-SOUND -- murmuration of thousands, sighing rush, tidal flat, winter. 2nd SCOLOPACIDAE. E=3. v1.49.309.

plumage.overall: Winter: gray-brown above, white below. Distinctive black belly patch (diagnostic when visible). Short, slightly decurved bill. Medium-small shorebird.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Black belly patch (breeding). Slightly decurved bill. In winter flocks on mudflats.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Breeding: rufous back, black belly patch prominent. Winter: gray-brown, belly patch reduced or absent. PNW birds are in winter plumage.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct flight in tight flocks. In murmuration: coordinated turning, shape-shifting, pulsating movements.

physical_description.overall: Length: 16-22 cm. Weight: 50-65g. Bill: slightly decurved, 3-4 cm. Small, compact shorebird.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Slightly decurved bill. Black belly (breeding). Tight flocking behavior.
physical_description.field_id: In winter: enormous flocks on mudflats. Gray-brown plumage. Slightly decurved bill. The murmuration itself is the identification -- no other PNW shorebird forms flocks of this size.

nodes.title: Murmuration -- Collective Aerial Display
nodes.description: Thousands of Dunlin wheeling in coordinated flight. Each bird responds to 6-7 nearest neighbors. No leader. Emergent coordination. Shape-shifting: ribbons, spheres, pulses. Turn waves propagate at 3x individual speed.

nodes.title: Collective Wing-Sound -- The Sighing Rush
nodes.description: Thousands of small wings beating and turning simultaneously create a rushing, sighing, whooshing collective sound. Not vocal. Atmospheric and enveloping. Rises and falls with flock movements. The voice of the flock itself.

nodes.title: Predator Defense -- The Falcon Trigger
nodes.description: Murmurations often triggered by Peregrine Falcon or Merlin. Predator cannot target individual in coordinated mass. Most dramatic displays are predator-driven. Defense through collective movement.

nodes.title: Tidal Flat Cycle -- Feed, Fly, Roost
nodes.description: Feeding on mudflats at low tide. Rising tide pushes flock to air -- triggering murmurations. Falling tide draws birds back to feed. The murmuration is the tidal transition. Winter phenomenon (Oct-Apr).

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE SCOLOPACIDAE! Snipe (168) to Dunlin (169). Both non-vocal sounds. Winnowing (mechanical, individual) to collective wing-sound (aggregate, flock). Solitary vs thousands.
CHARADRIIFORMES! Three consecutive Charadriiformes (167-169). Plover vocal alarm > snipe mechanical winnowing > sandpiper collective murmuration. Increasing abstraction from individual call.
FLOCK SPECIES! Both form large flocks. California Quail coveys (20-50). Dunlin flocks (5,000-50,000+). Scale difference is extreme. Both flock for predator defense.
NON-VOCAL COMPARISON! Grouse: wing percussion (individual). Dunlin: collective wing-sound (flock). Both produce sound through wings. Individual instrument vs collective orchestra.
